text: This is the world's tiniest pasta, but it's not for eating. It's 200 times thinner than a human hair, and it's really made of all-purpose flour. This microscopic web creates gaps that are big enough to let water through, but too small for bacteria. Plus, it has a similar structure to this, the structural network around your living cells. Which scientists think means it'll encourage cells to heal more quickly, so they hope to one day use it for bandages. They made it by shooting a flower solution at a metal sheet with an electric field between the two. The voltage causes the droplets to create these extremely thin pasta strands that dry mid-air and build up in layers, creating a sheet that looks like this. If you need more optimistic science and tech stories, follow along.
